title: Ballistic Exciton Flow Driven by Intertwined Exciton-Electron Orders in a Moiré Superlattice
authors: Shibin Deng, Jonas M. Peterson, Jonas Reimann, Heonjoon Park, Ammon Fischer, Takashi Taniguchi, Kenji Watanabe, Xiaodong Xu, Dante M. Kennes, Libai Huang
abstract: Moiré superlattices of transition-metal dichalcogenides (TMDs) host strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures in which bosonic excitons coexist with correlated electron lattices. Using ultrafast, time- and energy-resolved photoluminescence (PL) and reflectance microscopy, we show that strong exciton-electron and exciton-exciton repulsion can enable collective ballistic exciton transport in a WSe$_2$/WS$_2$ heterobilayer. The ballistic transport is energy-selective: repulsive interactions drive excitons into a higher moiré exciton band, where enhanced intersite hopping enables rapid spatial expansion. Correspondingly, the exciton mean-squared displacement (MSD) exhibits a quadratic time dependence ($\propto t^2$). This ballistic expansion is enhanced at fractional electron fillings where the electrons form generalized Wigner-crystal (GWC) orders. Afterwards, the system transitions into a mixed electron-exciton Mott state as Auger recombination and density depletion conclude the ballistic expansion. A one-dimensional Bose-Fermi Hubbard model solved using density-matrix renormalization group (DMRG) qualitatively reproduces the measured exciton transport and time-dependent response. It further confirms that strong cross-species interactions allow the electron crystal to perforate the exciton Mott background, accelerating its melting and enhancing exciton motion. Our results establish moiré TMDs as highly tunable platforms for realizing strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures, which we employ here to demonstrate real-time control of intertwined bosonic and electronic order and to establish a route to the exciton insulator-fluid transition.
summary: This paper investigates the collective transport of excitons in TMD moiré superlattices. The researchers show that repulsive interactions between excitons and electrons drive a ballistic expansion of excitons, particularly when electrons form Wigner-crystal orders. Using both ultrafast microscopy and DMRG simulations, they demonstrate how the electron crystal can accelerate the melting of the exciton Mott background. This work establishes moiré materials as a versatile platform for studying strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixtures.
main_problem: Understanding how the interplay between bosonic excitons and correlated electron lattices in TMD moiré superlattices drives collective transport and phase transitions.
main_result: Strong exciton-electron and exciton-exciton repulsions enable energy-selective ballistic exciton transport, which is enhanced at fractional electron fillings associated with generalized Wigner-crystal orders.
method: Ultrafast, time- and energy-resolved photoluminescence (PL) and reflectance microscopy combined with 1D Bose-Fermi Hubbard model simulations using DMRG.
model_or_system: A WSe2/WS2 heterobilayer moiré superlattice acting as a strongly interacting Bose-Fermi mixture where bosonic excitons coexist with correlated electron lattices.
key_observables: Exciton mean-squared displacement (MSD), time-dependent photoluminescence, and reflectance microscopy signals.
important_parameters: Fractional electron fillings, exciton-electron repulsion, exciton-exciton repulsion, and intersite hopping rates.
main_assumptions: The 1D Bose-Fermi Hubbard model qualitatively captures the complex 2D moiré physics.
paper_structure: The paper presents experimental observations of ballistic exciton transport via microscopy, analyzes the time-dependent MSD, relates the transport to electron filling and Wigner-crystal formation, and validates these findings with DMRG simulations of a Bose-Fermi Hubbard model.
why_interesting: It demonstrates real-time control of intertwined bosonic and electronic orders and provides a platform for studying the transition between exciton insulator and fluid phases in a highly tunable many-body system.
analysis_basis: abstract only; PDF text extraction failed or PDF unavailable
